title: FTC Tightens Antitrust Watch on Pharma as Patent Expiries Loom for Blockbuster Drugs Including Keytruda,...

contentHtml:
Philadelphia: The ŌĆŗU.S. Federal Trade Commission is on the lookout for anticompetitive practices in the pharmaceutical industry with a number of blockbuster drugs near the end of their patents, the FTC's head of antitrust enforcement said on Tuesday, as the ŌĆīagency looks to ŌüĀprotect consumers from ŌüĀunfair prices.

Monitoring the shift in the market brought about by patent expirations is part of the FTC's "laser focus" ŌĆŗon healthcare, Dan Guarnera, director of the FTC's bureau of competition, said at Reuters Events' Pharma USA conference in ŌĆŗPhiladelphia.

"We are always happy to hear concerns from market participants, including generics and patient groups to make sure that the entry of generics can happen as it's designed to under the patent ŌĆŗlaws," he said.

Many of the top-selling U.S. drugs are set ŌüĀto lose ŌĆītheir patent exclusivity by the end of the decade, including Merck's cancer drug ŌĆŗKeytruda, Bristol Myers ŌĆŗSquibb and Pfizer's blood thinner Eliquis and J&J's cancer drug Darzalex.

Healthcare is an ŌüĀintentional focus area for the FTC, which has moved to block ŌĆŗmultiple deals in the sector, including a $356 million medical equipment ŌĆŗmerger that was abandoned on Monday.

"It's an area that we care a lot about, not only because of obviously the huge effect it has on the economy, but also because it has such a direct effect on Americans' pocketbooks and well-being," Guarnera said. He added that he was speaking for himself, and that his comments may not reflect the FTC's views.

On Monday, Switzerland-based Alcon ŌĆīsaid it had abandoned its bid to acquire Lensar after the FTC indicated it intended to sue to block the deal. The acquisition would have reduced competition ŌĆŗbetween the two ŌĆŗcompanies, which both make laser ŌüĀsystems used in a specific type of cataract surgery, the FTC said.

The agency was concerned the deal would not only raise the cost of the systems but also would slow innovation, Guarnera said ŌĆŗon Tuesday.

The FTC is also attuned to how pharmaceutical mergers can affect incentives to innovate, even in deals where the drugs at issue are still in the pipeline stage, he said.

"We really do care about and want to see the effects of a merger on the firm's incentives to innovate, to continue their research and development programs and continue their pipeline drug production," Guarnera said.
